APPLICABLE LAW

The applicable law is found in Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) (“the Amendment Act”) came into effect on 6 May 2024 and made substantive amendments to the previous Act which apply to these proceedings. In determining parenting matters under Part VII of the Act the Court must still regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration: s 60CA.
The objects of Part VII are set out at s 60B:
60B Objects of Part

The objects of this Part are:
(a) to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety; and

(b) to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child done at New York on 20 November 1989.

In regard to parental responsibility, the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility as created by the previous s 61DA of the Act has been abolished. Instead, s 61D(3) of the Act provides the Court with the ability to deal with the allocation of responsibility for making decisions in relation to children as follows:
61D Parenting orders and parental responsibility

...
(3) A parenting order that deals with the allocation of responsibility for making decisions about major long-term issues in relation to the child (see subsection 64B(3)) may provide for joint or sole decision-making in relation to all or specified major long-term issues.
Section 61DAA of the Act provides the Court with an understanding of what joint decision‑making about major long-term issues entails:
61DAA Effect of parenting order that provides for joint decision-making about major long-term issues
(1) If a parenting order provides for joint decision-making by persons in relation to all or specified major long-term issues in relation to a child, then, except to the extent the order otherwise specifies, the order is taken to require each of the persons:
(a) to consult each other person in relation to each such decision; and

(b) to make a genuine effort to come to a joint decision.
(2) To avoid doubt, this section does not require any other person to establish, before acting on a decision about the child communicated by one of those persons, that the decision has been made jointly.
Subject to any order to the contrary, s 61DAB clarifies that if a child is spending time with a person under a parenting order, the order is not taken to require that person to consult with a person who has parental responsibility about decisions that are made in relation to the child that are not major long-term issues.
Because s 60CA and s 60B refer to the best interests of the child, the Court must consider s 60CC which specifies how the Court determines what is in a child’s best interests:
60CC How a court determines what is in a child’s best interests

Determining child's best interests
(1) Subject to subsection (4), in determining what is in the child’s best interests, the court must:
(a) consider the matters set out in subsection (2); and
(b) if the child is an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander child—also consider the matters set out in subsection (3).
General considerations
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and
(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

(Emphasis in original)
It should further be noted that due to the repeal of the former s 65DAA of the Act, there is no longer the statutory requirement for the Court consider equal time or substantial and significant time. Cases that refer to this section will need to be carefully considered as to whether they still apply in limited ways, if at all.
The definition of family violence is found in s 4AB of the Act, reproduced below:
4AB Definition of family violence etc.
(1) For the purposes of this Act, family violence means violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member), or causes the family member to be fearful.

(2) Examples of behaviour that may constitute family violence include (but are not limited to):

(a) an assault; or

(b) a sexual assault or other sexually abusive behaviour; or

(c) stalking; or

(d) repeated derogatory taunts; or

(e) intentionally damaging or destroying property; or

(f) intentionally causing death or injury to an animal; or
(g) unreasonably denying the family member the financial autonomy that he or she would otherwise have had; or

(h) unreasonably withholding financial support needed to meet the reasonable living expenses of the family member, or his or her child, at a time when the family member is entirely or predominantly dependent on the person for financial support; or

(i) preventing the family member from making or keeping connections with his or her family, friends or culture; or

(j) unlawfully depriving the family member, or any member of the family member’s family, of his or her liberty.

(3) For the purposes of this Act, a child is exposed to family violence if the child sees or hears family violence or otherwise experiences the effects of family violence.

(4) Examples of situations that may constitute a child being exposed to family violence include (but are not limited to) the child:

(a) overhearing threats of death or personal injury by a member of the child’s family towards another member of the child’s family; or

(b) seeing or hearing an assault of a member of the child’s family by another member of the child’s family; or

(c) comforting or providing assistance to a member of the child’s family who has been assaulted by another member of the child’s family; or

(d) cleaning up a site after a member of the child’s family has intentionally damaged property of another member of the child’s family; or

(e) being present when police or ambulance officers attend an incident involving the assault of a member of the child’s family by another member of the child’s family.

The recent amendments to the Act

The new legislative focus is on promoting the safety of children and carers, whereas the former provisions focused on protection from harm. The legislature must have intended there to be a distinction between the former provisions, which focused on protection from harm, and the current provisions which focus on the promotion of safety, otherwise the amendments would lack utility (Melounis & Melounis (No 4) [2024] FedCFamC1F 778). The amendments require a more nuanced, proactive and future-focused approach to considering the safety of children and those who care for them. This will be guided by historical risk assessment.
As stated by Campton J in Barnabas & Phineus [2025] FedCFamC1A 215 at [17]:
The construction of Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) after the 2024 amendments reinforces the consideration of the safety of children or another person as a focused starting point in those considerations to be weighed in determining a parenting regime that best promotes the best interests of the children (Pierce & Pierce (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1A 162 at [31]).

Emphasis added
Acknowledging this, the Court cautions against lapsing into simplistic binaries such as contending that promoting safety is always different from protection from harm. The reality is much more nuanced. Every case is different. Perhaps in many cases it will make no difference which approach is adopted, but in other cases the nuanced, proactive and future-focused difference is very important to the child and carer.

The case law

An interim hearing proceeds on evidence that is yet to be tested. There is no cross-examination to establish the veracity of the evidence before the Court. As the Full Court observed in Franklyn & Franklyn  [2019] FamCAFC 256 at [73]:
In interlocutory hearings, to the extent it is possible, judges are enjoined to make decisions about interim orders based on agreed facts, less contentious evidence, and inferences which fairly arise (citation omitted), but decisions must still be made despite contentious evidence. Judges are obliged to act on the strength of the evidence presented and, if it is relatively weak, are entitled to treat it so.
In relation to findings of fact during interlocutory hearings, Campton J observed in Walshe & Walshe [2025] FedCFamC1A 231:
A primary judge is not prohibited, despite constraints preventing the evidence being tested, from making contested factual determinations in an interlocutory Pt VII hearing.
Provisions of the Act now prescribe that a Court should not make orders for a child to spend time with a parent unless it is satisfied that it is safe to do so (s 60CC(2)(a), s 60CC(2)(e), s 60CC(2A) and s 60CG). These provisions mandate that a judge must engage with credible evidence of risks of harm to which a child might be exposed and against which they require protection, thereby making the evaluation of the contended risks an indispensable product of the interlocutory determination, despite evidence being contentious (Fowler & Northwood [2022] FedCFamC1A 173; (2022) FLC 94-114 at [24]). The assessment of risk is an evidence-based conclusion, not the product of an exercise of discretion (Isles at [85]).
Ensuring that competing claims as to controversial assertions are not ignored when considering an order that promotes the best interests of a child on an interlocutory basis engages nuances in evaluating the nature and quality of the evidence, including whether a primary or subsidiary fact is disputed, and the premise underscoring that dispute. Simply because evidence is disputed does not render it not credible or unreliable. These evaluations direct the process of weighing the probability of the competing factual disputes.
As explained by the Full Court in Marvel & Marvel (No 2) [2010] FamCAFC 101; (2010) 43 Fam LR 348 at [120], it is important to recognise that interim orders are only a waypoint on the road to a final solution for the family unit.
As has been frequently emphasised interim parenting proceedings, and orders made as a consequence, are a necessary but temporary measure until all the evidence can be tested, evaluated and weighed at a final hearing by the making of final parenting orders. Decisions judicial officers have to make in interim proceedings are difficult and, often for very good reason, a conservative approach, or one which is likely to avoid harm to a child is adopted. This is often to the understandable distress of a party who may not achieve the outcome he or she desires or thinks to be in the best interests of their child or children.

Restraints

In Hartley & Hartley [2021] FedCFamC1F 178 (“Hartley”), McClelland DCJ restated the relevant principles for the granting of injunctions:
The Court is empowered to grant injunctive relief in the nature of that sought by the parties pursuant to s 68B of the Act which relevantly empowers the Court to ‘grant such injunction as it considers appropriate for the welfare of the child’ including, pursuant to s 68B(2), by interlocutory order ‘in any case in which it appears to the court to be just or convenient to do so.’
At the outset it should be noted that the granting of an injunction restraining the exercise of what otherwise would be a person’s inherent rights and freedoms is a serious matter. The breach of such injunction potentially exposes a person to an action for contempt of Court. As noted by the plurality in Cardile v LED Builders Pty Limited [1999] HCA 18 (‘Cardile’) at [31]:
...that the court may grant an injunction in all cases in which it appears to the court to be just and convenient to do so – does not confer an unlimited power to grant injunctive relief. Regard must still be had to the existence of a legal or equitable right which the injunction protects against invasion or threatened invasion, or other unconscientious conduct or exercise of legal or equitable rights.

(references omitted)
In considering the nature of the injunctive relief, it is important to be aware of the general principle that ‘equity intervenes to the minimum extent necessary to do justice’.1
In the context of family law, in Sieling and Sieling [1979] FamCA 23; (1979) FLC 90-627 at 78 264, the Full Court said:

The power to grant injunctions is, of course, a discretionary power, not to be exercised lightly. The Court must balance the hardship to each party of granting or refusing an order and frame its order in such a way to impose no further restriction than is necessary to achieve the protection of the applicant’s interest.
FN1 See Giumelli v Giumelli [1999] HCA 10; (1999) 196 CLR 101 at [10] per Gleeson CJ, McHugh, Gummow, Kirby and Callinan JJ referred to in Norton & Locke [2013] FamCAFC 202; (2013) 284 FLR 51 at [72].

ISSUES

The following issues require determinations:
With whom the child ought to live;
What time the child ought to spend with the non‑resident parent;
Whether the parents should be subject to further restraints;
Whether the father should be subjected to further testing; and
Whether the father ought to be permitted to travel internationally with the child.

THE CASES PRESENTED BY THE PARTIES

The mother

The mother asserted that her application is supported by the recommendations as contained in the Expert Report. The Expert opined that the child ought to return to the primary care of the mother should her bail conditions allow and spend four nights per fortnight with the father (Expert Report, paragraph 306(d)-(e)). Further, the mother contended that the child has been exposed to extreme denigration of the mother by the father and within his household, the care of the child is being outsourced to the father’s adult daughter, Ms L, and that the father has had minimal involvement in the child’s ongoing treatment. It is the mother’s evidence that her bail conditions have been varied such that she is now residing in the Sydney area and is within “walking distance from [the child’s] school” (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 10).

Denigration occurring in the father’s household

The Court was taken to the following paragraphs of the Expert Report which identifies remarks from the child about his knowledge of the parental dispute:
When asked who the child used to live with, he responded “My Mum. But I don’t like living with her because she did drugs. She did [...]” and when asked how he knew about this he responded “Because my Dad told me. And she got in big trouble.” (Expert Report, paragraph 147);
The child remarked “My Dad said that my Mum is dumb. My Dad said that my Mum said to me that I am autistic. My Dad said to me that my Mum is sick in her brain” (Expert Report, paragraph 148);
The child said “I don’t have a passport. Because my Mum is not signing it. So, the lawyers are doing it. The Police” (Expert Report, paragraph 159). It was contended by the mother that the child’s statement in relation to his passport was as a result of the father discussing the proceedings with the child; and
The child advised the Expert, “My Mum also lies as well”. When asked how he knew this, he responded: “Because my Dad told me”. When the Expert enquired what the mother lied about, the child said “She says I am autistic but I’m not”. The child understands the word “Autistic” to mean: “It means that she thinks that I am sick” and that he knew this “Because my Dad told me” (Expert Report, paragraph 164).
In further support of the alleged denigration experienced in the father’s household, the mother directed the Court to paragraphs 176 – 177 of the Expert Report which confirms that Ms L was “not surprise[d]” to hear about the child recounting that the father told him that the mother lies and further, that the mother told the child he was “autistic and sick as she had heard him say that too”. The father was asked about his comments to the child by the Expert and he conceded that he had said “some” of the statements because “he needs to know what happened” (Expert Report, paragraph 201).
The mother contends that the father’s denigration of her in the presence of the child has caused psychological harm and that this should weigh in favour of making the orders sought by her.

Delegation of parental duties

The mother asserts that as between June 2025 to present, the father has outsourced “a large portion of the parenting of [the child]... including her involvement in his schooling, his therapies such as speech therapy and the general parenting” (Outline of Case Document of the mother filed 16 March 2026, paragraph 17 (“mother’s case outline”)). The role that Ms L plays in the care of the child is outlined from paragraphs 182 – 186 of the Expert Report.

The father’s lack of involvement in the child’s therapy

The mother asserts that the father unilaterally caused the following to be cancelled: all of the child’s therapies, NDIS funding and ongoing participation in the scheme, learning supports outside of school and all extra-curricular activities (mother’s affidavit, paragraph 46). The mother asserts that various professionals have recommended that the child attend upon an Occupational Therapist. Despite this, the father expressed an opposition to the child engaging in Occupational Therapy on the basis that it was not specifically recommended by Dr N, Psychologist who prepared a Report on 10 August 2025 (“[Dr N’s] Report”) (Exhibit A3). During oral submissions, it was contended that the father’s agreement for the child to now engage in Occupational Therapy has only arisen under the scrutiny of the Court. In respect to the cost of any future therapy, the mother contends that the father ought to cover the same in full due to cancelling the NDIS funding which previously enabled the child’s attendance upon therapists. The Court observes however, that the mother did not seek such an order in her proposal before the Court.

Potential risk to physical safety in the care of the father

The mother alleges that in January 2026, the child disclosed that the father has hit him in the face “multiple times” (mother’s case outline, paragraph 15). The Court notes that minimal context or explanation of how this allegation arose was provided by the mother.

Hair follicle testing

The mother pressed for orders which required the father to undertake hair follicle testing. The mother asserted that she had concerns relating to the father’s drinking in circumstances where a medical consultation from 19 April 2025 noted that the father was possibly experiencing gout (Exhibit A5). It was asserted that the father had previously experienced gout after “big nights of drinking” (Exhibit A5).

Restraint as sought by the Independent Children’s Lawyer

International travel

In support of these concerns the mother provided text messages between the parents dated 20 March 2023 where the father provides the mother with a link to Q School in City R and says “Great school” and further provided an undated text message from the father which reads “If you’re prepared to give up, I will raise him. But it won’t be here” (Exhibit A6). The father

In response to the submissions advanced by the mother, the father asserted that the present interim arrangements for the child to live with the father were made by the consent on 11 July 2025 which was a time where the Expert Report was available to them. The only change in circumstance identified by the father was the variation of the mother’s bail conditions which occurred merely one week after the consent orders, and yet she waited until January 2026 to bring an application. Further, it is contended by the father that the upheaval in the child’s life has been caused by significant miscarriages in the mother’s judgment, and that these parents did not require the intervention of the Court for parenting arrangements prior to the mother’s criminal conduct. The father asserts that the genesis of his concern in relation to the mother is certainly warranted, particularly when viewing the photographs of the drugs and drug paraphernalia littered throughout the house where the child was living with the mother (Exhibit R5). In the alternative, the father submitted that the effect on the child would be more manageable in circumstances where he was merely spending time with the mother and she became incarcerated. Delegation of parental duties

The father denied delegating his parental responsibility to Ms L. He described her assistance as a “nanny like capacity” which is necessary where he is a working parent. It is asserted that the assistance offered by Ms L is a bonus to the child as he is surrounded by family members as opposed to a hired “nanny”.

Nevertheless, the father has sought scaffolded Court orders which require him to continue the child’s engagement in therapies and for the child to undertake a further assessment by P Health Services (the father’s proposal, Orders 6–8).

Potential risk to physical safety in the care of the father

The father denies the allegation that he has physically hit the child and that if the mother held a genuine belief that he is a risk to the child that she would not seek that the child spend four nights per fortnight unsupervised with him.

Hair follicle testing

The father contended that should the Court order that he complete a hair follicle test, he would comply with the same. However, the father asserts that the evidence propounded in support of such an order would not compel the Court to make the same.

Restraints as sought by the Independent Children’s Lawyer

The father supported the restraint on the mother allowing any person, other than maternal family members, in her house when caring for the child. It was submitted by the father that the mother’s previous conduct has been so significant that the orders made ought not allow for her judgment to miscarry again, given that it is alleged the mother engaged in the sale of drugs from her home. In response to the argument raised by the mother as to the practical limitations for the child imposed by the restraint, the father contended that any “play days” could operate from a public place.

International Travel

The father concedes that the orders sought for travel are not in response to any specific trip. However, the father asserts that the child would benefit from being able to travel for the purpose of a holiday with his paternal family (Outline of Case Document filed 16 March 2026 by the father, paragraph 8). In response to the concerns raised by the mother, the father asserts that he has no intention to relocate with the child, he is not a foreign national and he has strong ties to Australia.

The Independent Children’s Lawyer

The Independent Children’s Lawyer did not advance oral submissions and sought to rely on her comprehensive case outline.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer does not support a change of residence for the child, however, is of the opinion that the child would benefit from spending further time in the mother’s care (the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s position, paragraphs 3–4).
In relation to the order sought relating to the father undergoing hair follicle testing, the Independent Children’s Lawyer contends that this order would provide clarity to the Court where the allegations of his alcohol consumption are ongoing (the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s position, paragraph 7).
The Independent Children’s Lawyer does not support an order for the child to travel internationally, at this time, as it is an issue to be ventilated at a final hearing and the maintenance of the relationship between the mother and child ought to be prioritised (the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s position, paragraph 23).

DISCUSSION AND ORDERS MADE

The Expert Report identifies deficits in the father’s parenting but does not satisfactorily address the mother’s alleged criminal activities and how this adversely reflects on her parenting capacity. The Expert did not have the evidence before him that the Court presently has in relation to the charges the mother is facing. In observations, however, the Expert formed the view that the child has a good relationship with both parents (Expert Report, paragraph 279). It is undisputed that the mother was the primary carer before separation and up and until her arrest in late 2024, where thereafter the father became, and continues to be, the child’s primary carer.
It is clear from the totality of the evidence, including the exhibits, that the child has special needs which need to be addressed both by his parents, and professionally. He presents with “Global Development Delay, expressive and receptive language delays and motor tics” and “currently meets the DSM-5 criteria for Autism Spectrum Disorder” (Exhibit A1). Dr B, Paediatrician has also indicated that the child meets the criteria for “ADHD predominantly inattentive type” (Exhibit ICL1). He does seem well engaged with his treating professionals, despite some disagreement between his parents about his diagnosis, treatment and prognosis. The latter is probably, and at least partially, consequential to the parental conflict manifested by inadequate communication, mutual suspicion, and lack of trust. The mother’s contention that the father is not meeting the child’s needs cannot be established without the testing of the evidence. The tendered evidence suggests that the child would benefit from minimal unnecessary change in his routine.
A strong impression is formed that denigration of the mother occurs in the father’s household with the likely source being the father, or his adult daughter who attends to the day-to-day care of the child. If this is occurring, it is completely inappropriate and must stop. The father would do well to remember that if he is found to be denigrating the mother at a final hearing, this will be a highly relevant factor for the Court. Additionally, the father is reminded of Order 2.1 of the orders made 11 July 2025.
The mother makes various allegations and aspersions that the child is harmed or neglected in the father’s care. No finding is possible at this stage. The evidence of the child’s good relationship with each parent may well be inconsistent with this allegation. This will be a matter for final hearing. Regardless, the parents are reminded that Order 2.6 of the orders made 11 July 2025 retrain the parents from any physical discipline of the child.
The mother was initially charged with several offences relating to prohibited drugs (Exhibit R2). The mother provided to the Court a notice of listing which indicates two charges are to be withdrawn (Exhibit A8). If this is correct, this leaves a few more charges.
The mother has plead not guilty. No trial date has been allocated. Beyond a mere assertion from the Bar table, there is no evidence that a non-custodial sentence would be sought by the prosecution. The mother, understandably, does not press for a hearing date in this Court, until the criminal trial is completed.
The exhibits indicate that the circumstances of the mother’s offending are very serious. Of particular concern to the Court is the allegation by the prosecution that prohibited drugs were manufactured in the home in which the mother lived with the child. Indeed, the mother admitted to police that there was an “[illicit substance]” (being the prohibited drug) inside of her home (Exhibit R2).
The Fact Sheet, which became Exhibit R2, refers to the “operational risks associated with [the drug] and an enclosed space”. This is the most likely explanation for the reference in Exhibit R2, a statement from a police officer relating to the search of the mother’s home, to the said police officer needing to obtain Personal Protective Equipment (“PPE”) before entering the premises to assist with search duties. This observation is strengthened by the reference at paragraph 7 of the said statement to the attendance of the Chemical Operations Unit and HAZMAT at the premises, due to the “large amount of [an illicit substance]” located throughout the premises. Moreover, Exhibit R5, presumably the photographs taken during the search of the premises, include photographs of what appears to be the child’s bedroom which seems to the littered with drug making paraphernalia. Whether the bedroom was in fact shared with the mother is irrelevant in the present context. The strong impression formed from this tendered material is that the mother physically exposed the child in a most serious manner to her alleged drug manufacture and sale activities. The present issue is parenting capacity, not criminality.
If the mother is convicted, and a custodial sentence is imposed, there will be yet another potentially significant change in the child’s life. This possibility, per se, does not contraindicate some further increase in the mother’s time as is proposed by both the father and the Independent Children’s Lawyer, but it (together with the safety issues discussed above) does militate against the drastic reversal of care arrangements proposed by the mother.
The mother relied on an affidavit from her treating psychiatrist, Dr C filed 20 February 2026 which contains a Report dated 12 February 2026 (“[Dr C’s] Report”). It paints a positive, but nonetheless guarded, picture of the mother’s mental health. She has been diagnosed with long‑standing Post Traumatic Stress Disorder (“PTSD”) arising from cumulative traumatic experiences across her lifetime, some of which predate the relationship with the father. At paragraphs 2.12 and 2.13 of Dr C’s Report, he provides a favourable overall psychiatric prognosis but warns that the mother continues to experience residual trauma related symptoms but is coping well given the “extensive litigation that I understand is occurring in the family law matter between the parties” (Dr C’s Report, paragraph 2.13).
It is odd, the Court tentatively observes, that even though Dr C was informed about the mother’s criminal proceedings he makes no reference to this in his Report. It would be a reasonable hypothesis that the criminal proceedings might adversely affect the mother’s mental health. The circumstances of the mother’s alleged criminal activity might also be relevant in a mental health report. The weight to be given to Dr C’s Report is limited by such omissions.
The mother’s contentions that the child is not safe in the father’s care ring hollow in circumstances where the Court is not satisfied that she presents the likelihood of greater safety in her care. In any event, she agreed to the existing spends time with arrangement. Were it not for the father’s proposal to expand the child’s time with the mother, the Court would not have contemplated this. The Court can only assume that despite the concerns that he has about the child safety in the mother’s care, he is nonetheless satisfied that any consequential risk is not unacceptable. Additional time may create an opportunity for a balancing of the father’s possible adverse narrative of the mother in his household. The Court notes that the Independent Children’s Lawyer proposed five nights per fortnight. In a finely balanced case, the Court is more comfortable with the four nights per fortnight as proposed by the father. Orders 2 and 3 of the father’s proposal will be made.
The father’s proposals for travel orders are hypothetical in the sense that no specific travel is planned, are therefore unnecessary, and constitute a distraction in these proceedings that is not welcomed by the Court. This issue can wait for another day. The Court declines to make Orders 4–5 and 9–12 of the father’s proposal as the child obtaining a passport as it is unnecessary given the denial of international travel.
Orders 6–8 of the father’s proposal will be made as it is essential that the child continue his attendance upon his paediatrician and speech pathologist. Requiring the father to enable the child’s attendance should assist with mediating some of the mother’s concerns relating to this topic. Further, the father and the Independent Children’s Lawyer contended that the child re‑engage occupational therapy with P Health Services. The mother contended that the child attend upon S Health Services. The Court is of the view that the child ought to attend upon P Health Services as this is where he was previously engaged and hence does not subject the child to further professionals.
In relation to the supply of the Expert Report to Dr B (the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s proposal, Order 7), the mother was in favour of same and sought an order in the same terms. It was highlighted by the mother that Dr B requested a copy of the Expert Report (Expert Report, paragraph 232). The father contended that the Expert Report was prepared for a forensic purpose and Dr B should not be invited into the legal space between the parents. The Court views that providing the Expert Report to Dr B would enable him to have a broader understanding of the child’s circumstances which may aid in his treatment, and thus Order 7 as sought by the Independent Children’s Lawyer will be made.
The Court declines to make orders requiring the father to undergo further hair follicle testing as the need was not made out on the evidence.
The Court has made Orders 11–13 proposed by the Independent Children’s Lawyer. Specifically in relation to Order 11, the Court is of the view that the mother ought to be subject to more stringent orders now that she will be spending increased time with the child. The impracticality concerns raised by the mother are easily overcome by facilitating time with third parties in public locations. Order 12 proposed by the Independent Children’s Lawyer limits the potential for parental conflict which is in the best interests of the child in the circumstances of this high conflict case. Lastly, the restraints surrounding the parents unilaterally acting in relation to the child’s treatment is essential in this case where the topic of the child’s therapy presents as a source of conflict between the parents, as well as the previous unilateral action of the father in cancelling the child’s engagement in therapies.

CONCLUSION

Orders will be made in accordance with my findings above. As the mother is not ready for a hearing date, this file will be returned to the case management judge at the earliest time.
